title: The Davis Wing, the B-24 Liberator, and the Self-Made Bet That Paid Off
gist: The B-24 Liberator, the most-produced US military aircraft of WWII, owed its speed and range edge to the Davis wing — a self-taught engineer's empirically-derived airfoil that credentialed institutions rejected, offering a defense-innovation parable of solution engineering running ahead of science.

insights.claim: The Davis wing was derived empirically by a self-taught 'practical engineer' who got the idea from watching falling water droplets assume a teardrop shape, patented as a 'fluid foil' in 1934, and its performance later proved to stem from extending laminar flow farther aft along the chord.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: Archetype of intuition-led innovation beating credentialed method — a recurring defense-tech investment lens.

insights.claim: Every institution Davis approached was skeptical because he lacked credentials, guarded his formula, and the wing resembled none of the 78 proven airfoils the National Advisory Committee on Aeronautics had wind-tunnel-tested and cataloged.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: Illustrates how institutional gatekeeping and secrecy nearly killed a superior technology.
insights.locator: But the idea faced heavy skepticism

insights.claim: The wing worked before the institutions of aeronautics could explain why — a case of solution engineering running ahead of science.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: Framework for backing empirically-validated capability even when theory lags.
insights.quote: The wing worked before the institutions of aeronautics could say why it worked — a case of solution engineering running ahead of science.

insights.claim: In August 1938 side-by-side high-fidelity wind-tunnel tests, the Davis wing showed 20 percent better performance than a comparable traditional airfoil, prompting Consolidated to fund a full-scale demonstrator.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: Quantifies the measurable edge that justified a capital bet on an unproven design.
insights.locator: When a new high-fidelity wind tunnel opened

insights.claim: American industry produced 18,482 B-24 Liberators in five years — the most-produced US military aircraft of WWII — and nearly 6,000 more than the B-17 despite the B-17's four-year production head start.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: Operational reach drove production demand, showing capability advantages compound into scale.
insights.locator: The most produced American military aircraft

insights.claim: The unusually thick Davis wing was counterintuitively efficient and, as a by-product, could carry more fuel than a conventional wing, giving the B-24 its faster, farther, heavier-load advantage over the B-17.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: A single design choice created the range that made the aircraft strategically valuable across all theaters.
insights.locator: There was one more important detail

insights.claim: Davis licensed the wing to Consolidated in 1938 for $2,500 per prototype plus a stepped royalty of one-half of one percent of each bomber's price, but as production exploded the US government intervened in April 1943 to cut his royalty to $5 per airplane, capping his total at about $400,000 (roughly $9 million today).
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: A historical case of government clawing back outsized IP economics under wartime volume — a risk for defense-IP monetization.

insights.claim: The P-51 Mustang independently used the NACA 45-100 laminar-flow airfoil in 1940, representing the research establishment's theory-driven answer to the same low-drag problem Davis reached empirically through intuition and secrecy.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: Shows empirical and institutional innovation paths converging on the same frontier simultaneously.
insights.locator: In 1939, a company called North American Aviation

insights.claim: The Davis wing never became a template — its wind-tunnel advantage was hard to reproduce operationally because small leading-edge roughness sharply increased drag, and faster swept and thinner wings soon overtook it.
insights.speaker: Mike Benitez
insights.so_what: A cautionary note that a lab edge can degrade in the field and be leapfrogged by newer approaches.
